doc.title: Liability Gain or Loss (Actuarial Assumptions)
doc.event: At year-end, Hollis Co.'s actuary revises key assumptions (discount rate, mortality), changing the projected benefit obligation by $25,000. Prepare the entry to record the liability gain or loss.

entries.label: PBO up $25,000 → loss, even though no cash moved

lines.why: A bigger obligation is a LOSS — the direction that hurts funded status.
lines.trap: No cash, no expense — assumption changes go to OCI and wait for the corridor test.

lines.why: The net pension liability grows with the PBO.
A smaller obligation improves the funded position.
The gain defers to OCI — same treatment as asset gains.

memorize.body: PBO up = loss. Assets up = gain. Whichever direction HURTS funded status is the loss.

questions.prompt: Assumption changes increase the PBO by $25,000. How much hits OCI—Gain/Loss this period?

distractors.feedback: The change is recognized NOW, in OCI — you don't wait for benefits to be paid.
You jumped to amortization. The full 25,000 lands in OCI first; corridor amortization (if any) starts NEXT year.

build.scaffold: Two lines. First decide gain or loss (does the change hurt funded status?), then OCI takes the income-side line.
